summary: Pitkin County's verified housing record centers on APCHA deed restrictions, the voter-approved 1.5-mill affordable and workforce housing levy, county STR licensing in unincorporated areas, the regional Lumberyard pipeline, a small 127-unit APCHA LIHTC footprint, and participation in WMRHC's Good Deeds buy-down program.

sections.heading: Aspen / Pitkin County Housing Authority (regional joint authority)

paragraphs.text: APCHA was created in 1982 by an Intergovernmental Agreement between the City of Aspen and Pitkin County as an independent multi-jurisdictional housing authority. APCHA describes itself as the oldest and largest mountain-resort workforce-housing program in North America.

APCHA now has an eight-member Board of Directors, with five voting members and three alternates. Aspen City Council and the Pitkin County Board of County Commissioners each hold one voting seat and one alternate, while the other four board members are appointed jointly. The county board page describes APCHA powers including investigating housing needs, issuing bonds, borrowing funds, securing mortgages, managing employee housing projects, and adopting annual rental and ownership qualifications.

APCHA oversees 1,652 deed-restricted ownership units and about 1,430 rental units. Ownership and rental units are organized in Categories 1 through 5 plus Resident Occupied (RO); Categories 6 and 7 have been rolled into Category 5. Deed restrictions typically cap appreciation at 3% per year or CPI, whichever is less, with approved capital improvements allowed under APCHA rules.

sections.heading: Issue 1A county property tax for housing

paragraphs.text: Pitkin County placed Issue 1A on the November 5, 2024 ballot to authorize a 1.5-mill ad valorem property tax for up to 25 years for affordable and attainable housing. Aspen Daily News reported the worker-housing 1A question was winning on election night, and later Aspen Times coverage states voters approved the mill levy in 2024. The county projected approximately $8.5M in annual revenue, with an estimated cost of about $121 per $1M of residential value and $435 per $1M of commercial value.

The mill levy collected $8,527,200 in 2025. Pitkin County spent $621,807 of that first-year revenue on housing efforts, left more than $7.9M unspent, expected roughly $8.3M in 2026 collections, planned about $11.2M of housing-mill-levy spending across 2025 and 2026 funds, budgeted $5M for Phillips Mobile Home Park water infrastructure, and set aside $4.5M for grants and contributions.

The county Employee Housing Impact Fee, last amended by BOCC Ordinance 03-2020 in February 2020, is recalculated annually using methodology tied to updated data points such as HUD median-income data, residential sales prices, and mortgage interest rates.

The Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit, 100% affordable rental development near the Airport Business Center along Highway 82, planned as three four-story buildings serving households from below 50% AMI up to 240% AMI under APCHA restrictions. Aspen selected Gorman & Company as development partner on July 14, 2025 and approved financial closing A by Resolution #042, Series 2026, on March 24, 2026. Phase 1 construction includes 104 units with occupancy planned for 2028; two later buildings totaling 173 units are planned for financial closing in May 2027 with occupancy in late 2028 and 2029.

On November 4, 2025, Aspen voters supported Issue 2B by 61.92% to 38.08%, authorizing up to $70M in revenue-backed debt for the Lumberyard project, with a maximum annual city repayment of $6.5M and total repayment cost not to exceed $152.55M.

Pitkin County requires short-term rentals in unincorporated areas to have a valid license. County rules include a four-night minimum, 120-night maximum, proof of short-term-rental activity between May 11, 2017 and May 11, 2022, and a tiered fee structure of 0.05%, 0.06%, or 0.07% of assessed home market value based on rental-night tier.

The Pitkin County Board of County Commissioners voted 3-1 on June 14, 2023 to prohibit STRs in the Rural Remote zone. The county ordinance text states that no short-term-rental license shall issue for any property in the Rural and Remote zone district.

APCHA-managed LIHTC inventory consists of 127 Low-Income Housing Tax Credit units: 40 at Aspen Country Inn and 87 at Truscott Phase II. The memo also describes CHFA as Colorado's housing credit agency and monitor for state LIHTC compliance.
Pitkin County is among Colorado's twelve rural-resort counties for which CHFA includes 130%, 140%, 150%, and 160% AMI rent and income limits in its 2025 tables for Proposition 123 programs.

The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC) was established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit serving the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys. Aspen Journalism reported that Pitkin County was among the coalition's founding government members.

WMRHC's Good Deeds program assists free-market homebuyers by contributing 30% of a purchase price in exchange for a deed restriction, with resale appreciation capped at 3% annually. Pitkin County approved a $1M grant in 2025 and later directed $2M over 2025 and 2026 from affordable-housing funds, following $1M of earlier support from ARPA and general-fund dollars.
